title: About Me
text:
My name is Sahil Ashish Bhattacharya. I am currently pursuing an MSc in Business Analytics at Dublin City University (DCU), Dublin, Ireland.
My academic journey has given me a strong foundation in quantitative methods, data-driven decision-making, and applied machine learning. I am particularly interested in how AI and ML can be deployed to address real business challenges — from customer retention strategy to natural language processing pipelines.
I enjoy working with data end-to-end: gathering and cleaning raw datasets, building and evaluating predictive models, and communicating insights clearly to both technical and non-technical audiences.

title: Cohort-Driven Ticket Sales & CRM Strategy for the Football Association of Ireland
text:
This project was completed as part of my MSc in Business Analytics at Dublin City University. Working within a team, we tackled a real-world CRM challenge facing the Football Association of Ireland (FAI): how to build genuine, long-term fan loyalty in a stadium environment where sell-out crowds can mask underlying weaknesses in independent fan retention.
My Role: Data Sourcing & Collection Lead

title: Cohort-Driven Ticket Sales & CRM Strategy for the Football Association of Ireland
text:
This project was completed as part of my MSc in Business Analytics at Dublin City University. Working within a team, we tackled a real-world CRM challenge facing the Football Association of Ireland (FAI): how to build genuine, long-term fan loyalty in a stadium environment where sell-out crowds can mask underlying weaknesses in independent fan retention.
My Role: Data Sourcing & Collection Lead

title: Cohort-Driven Ticket Sales & CRM Strategy for the Football Association of Ireland
text:
The Business Problem
Aviva Stadium regularly achieves high attendance figures, which on the surface suggests strong demand. However, sell-out events can obscure an important risk: much of the attendance may be driven by transactional, occasion-based purchasing rather than deep, loyalty-driven fan relationships.
A key insight we identified early in the project was the youth pipeline problem. A significant proportion of younger attendees arrive not as independent fans, but through their parents or via youth football participation. This makes their ticket purchases conditional — tied to family decisions and youth team involvement rather than a personal, independent attachment to the national team. When those pathways fall away (e.g., a child ages out of youth football, a parent stops attending), there is no guarantee the attendee converts into a loyal adult fan.
This distinction — between transactional attendance and relationship-driven loyalty — became the central analytical lens for the project.

title: Cohort-Driven Ticket Sales & CRM Strategy for the Football Association of Ireland
text:
Data Sourcing & Collection
My primary contribution was leading the data sourcing and collection strategy — thinking through what data would be needed to properly model fan retention, age-based cohort behaviour, and customer lifetime value in the FAI context.
Key data dimensions I identified as essential for a full CRM analysis:

Ticket purchase history — repeat purchase rates, inter-match gap analysis, cohort retention curves
Age at first purchase — to model fan acquisition age and long-term retention probability
Youth football registration linkage — to identify attendees whose engagement is mediated by club or school participation
Geographic data — to understand regional fan density and distance-decay effects on repeat attendance
Communication touchpoint data — email open rates, campaign engagement, conversion to purchase

One of the core challenges I highlighted is that much of this data exists across siloed systems — ticketing platforms, grassroots registration databases, email marketing tools — and would require careful integration before any segmentation or predictive modelling could be applied.

title: Cohort-Driven Ticket Sales & CRM Strategy for the Football Association of Ireland
text:
Analytical Approach
Based on the data framework I outlined, the team developed a cohort-based CRM strategy structured around three fan segments:








Segment
Description
CRM Priority




Youth-linked
Attending via parent/youth club
Convert to independent fans before pathway ends


Lapsed adult
Previously regular, no recent purchase
Re-engagement campaigns, targeted offers


Loyal adult
Consistent repeat purchasers
Retention, upsell, advocacy programmes



A key recommendation was to begin tracking age-based drop-off points — the ages at which youth-linked attendees either convert to independent loyalty or disengage entirely — and to use this to trigger proactive CRM outreach at the right moment.

title: Cohort-Driven Ticket Sales & CRM Strategy for the Football Association of Ireland
text:
Reflection
This project fundamentally changed how I think about data collection decisions and their downstream consequences.
Before this project, I thought of data sourcing as a preparatory step — something you do before the “real” analysis begins. Working on this project showed me that data collection is the analysis, in an important sense. The decision of what to collect, and what not to collect, directly shapes what questions you can later answer. In the FAI context, a CRM team that only tracks whether tickets were sold — without tracking who bought them, why, and how they were connected to the game — is systematically blind to its most important retention risks.
Understanding the youth cohort dynamic was particularly instructive. It illustrated how aggregate metrics (sell-out rate, total attendance) can be misleading if they are not disaggregated by behavioural segment. A stadium that is always full is not necessarily building a loyal fanbase — it may simply be drawing on a large pool of occasional or event-driven visitors who will not return consistently.
This project also improved my thinking about stakeholder needs in analytics. The FAI’s CRM challenge is not purely a data problem; it is a business strategy problem with a data component. Framing the analysis around the organisation’s actual strategic objective — building independent, long-term fan loyalty — kept the work grounded and relevant, rather than technical for its own sake.
I came away with a much stronger appreciation of how data collection strategy, business problem framing, and CRM design are inseparable in applied analytics work.

title: Cohort-Driven Ticket Sales & CRM Strategy for the Football Association of Ireland
text:
Tools & Methods

Python (pandas, matplotlib, seaborn)
Cohort analysis framework
Customer segmentation logic
CRM strategy mapping
Data architecture planning (ticketing, grassroots, comms systems)

title: Reflection
text:
Building this portfolio website has been one of the more unexpectedly rewarding experiences of my MSc in Business Analytics at Dublin City University. Coming from a background with a growing interest in technology and data, I had some familiarity with analytical tools — but creating and publishing a personal website from scratch was an entirely new challenge.
The process began with getting to grips with Quarto — a publishing framework I had not worked with before. Understanding the relationship between .qmd source files, the _quarto.yml configuration, and the final rendered HTML output required patience and careful reading of documentation. Early on, small configuration errors caused the entire site to fail to render, which quickly taught me the value of incremental changes and version control.
One of the most valuable aspects of this project was learning to think about content structurally. Deciding what to include on each page — and how to organise the navbar, footer, and project listings — forced me to consider the audience: recruiters, academic supervisors, and future collaborators. This perspective directly influenced the writing across the site, pushing me toward a tone that is clear, professional, and purposeful.
Working with GitHub for deployment also deepened my understanding of version control workflows. Committing changes, pushing to a remote repository, and enabling GitHub Pages as the publishing source were skills that had previously felt abstract but now feel genuinely practical — and directly transferable to collaborative data projects in a professional setting.
The technical challenges — fixing broken file paths, correctly linking project pages, ensuring consistent rendering — closely mirrored real-world data pipeline problems: small upstream errors cascade into downstream failures, and systematic debugging is always more efficient than guesswork.
Overall, this project has strengthened my technical confidence and produced a professional artefact I am proud to share. It has also reinforced a broader lesson I am taking from my MSc: the ability to communicate analytical work clearly — to structure, present, and contextualise findings — is just as important as the analysis itself.

title: BERT vs Generative AI for Text Classification
text: This project was completed as part of my MSc in Business Analytics at Dublin City University. The assessment involved working in a Jupyter notebook environment to implement and evaluate two distinct approaches to text classification: a traditional BERT-based transformer model and a Generative AI approach, comparing their performance, workflow characteristics, and practical trade-offs.

title: BERT vs Generative AI for Text Classification
text: This project was completed as part of my MSc in Business Analytics at Dublin City University. The assessment involved working in a Jupyter notebook environment to implement and evaluate two distinct approaches to text classification: a traditional BERT-based transformer model and a Generative AI approach, comparing their performance, workflow characteristics, and practical trade-offs.

title: BERT vs Generative AI for Text Classification
text:
Background
Text classification is a foundational NLP task with wide business applications — from sentiment analysis of customer feedback to automated document routing, spam filtering, and topic categorisation. Two major paradigms have emerged for tackling these tasks:

BERT-style discriminative models — fine-tuned transformer encoders that learn to classify input text directly into predefined categories. Well-suited to structured, supervised classification with labelled training data.
Generative AI approaches — large language models prompted to perform classification through instruction following, few-shot prompting, or zero-shot reasoning. Require no fine-tuning but rely on prompt design and model capability.

Comparing these approaches — not just on accuracy, but on interpretability, resource requirements, workflow design, and business fit — was the core analytical challenge of this project.

title: BERT vs Generative AI for Text Classification
text:
Methodology

BERT-Based Classification
The BERT pipeline involved:

Tokenisation using the Hugging Face transformers library (bert-base-uncased)
Fine-tuning on a labelled text dataset with a classification head
Evaluation using accuracy, F1 score, and confusion matrix analysis
Training via Google Colab with GPU runtime to manage compute requirements



Generative AI Approach
The generative AI pipeline involved:

Zero-shot and few-shot prompting to elicit classifications from a large language model
Prompt engineering to structure inputs consistently and extract structured label outputs
Evaluation on the same held-out test set for direct comparison with BERT

title: BERT vs Generative AI for Text Classification
text:
Key Findings








Dimension
BERT
Generative AI




Accuracy (test set)
Strong on in-distribution data
Competitive on ambiguous cases


Training required
Yes — labelled data needed
No — zero-shot capable


Interpretability
Moderate (attention weights)
Low (black-box prompting)


Latency
Fast at inference
Slower per call


Workflow complexity
Higher (fine-tuning pipeline)
Lower (prompt-based)


Best fit
High-volume, structured tasks
Exploratory or low-resource settings



The comparison revealed that model choice is not just an accuracy decision — it is a workflow design decision that depends on the volume of labelled data available, the required inference speed, the interpretability demands of the use case, and the cost of model deployment.

title: BERT vs Generative AI for Text Classification
text:
Reflection
This project had a significant impact on how I think about model selection in applied NLP.
Going into it, I expected the comparison to be straightforward: BERT would win on accuracy for a structured classification task, and generative AI would be less precise but more flexible. What I found was more nuanced. Generative AI approaches performed surprisingly well on ambiguous or edge-case examples where context and semantic understanding mattered more than pattern matching on labelled training examples. BERT, by contrast, was more consistent and reliable on clear, in-distribution cases where sufficient labelled data had been provided.
More importantly, I came to understand that the framing of “which model is better” is often the wrong question. The better question is: better for what, in what context, with what constraints? A business deploying a customer service routing tool at scale needs speed, consistency, and low operational cost — BERT fine-tuned on their specific categories is likely the right tool. An analyst quickly exploring the themes in a new corpus of documents, without labelled data, may get more value from a generative AI approach in hours rather than weeks.
Working inside a Jupyter notebook also reinforced the importance of clear, reproducible experimental structure. Tracking experiments, documenting preprocessing decisions, and presenting evaluation results in a readable format are skills I actively practised and improved through this project.
The project deepened my interest in NLP and applied machine learning considerably. It also gave me a much more grounded understanding of the practical engineering trade-offs involved in deploying language models — something that is increasingly relevant for anyone working in business analytics as AI tools become standard components of the analytics stack.

title: BERT vs Generative AI for Text Classification
text:
Tools & Methods

Python (transformers, torch, scikit-learn, pandas, numpy)
Hugging Face bert-base-uncased
Jupyter Notebook / Google Colab
Zero-shot and few-shot prompting
F1 score, accuracy, confusion matrix evaluation
